text:
Which of the following reactions produce ethyl cyanide as a major product?

1. $$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH=NOH} \xrightarrow{(\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O}}$$
2. $$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{Ethanolic KCN}}$$
3. $$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{Ethanolic AgCN}}$$
4. $$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CON(CH}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{SO}_2\text{Cl, Pyridine, 70°C}}$$
explanation:
**Reaction 1:** Beckmann rearrangement of oxime using (CH₃CO)₂O:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH=NOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CN (ethyl cyanide)}$$ ✓

**Reaction 2:** SN2 reaction of 1° alkyl halide with KCN:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl} + \text{KCN} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CN (major)} + \text{KCl}$$ ✓

**Reaction 3:** Ethanolic AgCN reacts with alkyl halides via **carbon attack** (SN1 tendency) to give predominantly the **isocyanide** (CH₃CH₂NC), not cyanide ✗

**Reaction 4:** This dehydration reaction of amide does not give ethyl cyanide in the expected manner ✗

**Reactions 1 and 2** give ethyl cyanide as the major product.
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 1

text:
The order of boiling points of the following compounds is:

1. $$(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{N}$$   2. $$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2$$   3. $$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NHCH}_3$$
explanation:
Boiling points of amines depend on **intermolecular forces** — primarily van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding.

- The dominant factor here is the **branching of the carbon chain**: more branching → more compact shape → less surface area → weaker van der Waals forces → lower boiling point

Analysing:
- **2 (n-propylamine, 1°):** Straight chain, maximum surface area, can form 2 N–H bonds → **highest boiling point** (~49°C)
- **3 (ethylmethylamine, 2°):** Some branching, can form 1 N–H bond → intermediate (~37°C)
- **1 (trimethylamine, 3°):** Most branched, compact structure, NO N–H bonds → **lowest boiling point** (~3°C)

**Boiling point order: 2 > 3 > 1**
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 2

text:
What will be product A in the following reaction?

$$\text{Aniline} + \text{HNO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{HCl/5°C}} A$$
explanation:
When aniline (primary aromatic amine) reacts with HNO₂ (generated from NaNO₂ + HCl) at **0–5°C**:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2 + \text{HNO}_2 + \text{HCl} \xrightarrow{0-5°\text{C}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{N}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Product A = Benzene diazonium chloride**

This reaction is called **diazotisation**. It must be carried out at 0–5°C because:
- At higher temperatures, the diazonium salt is unstable and decomposes
- At low temperatures, the diazonium salt is stable and can be used for further reactions
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 3

text: Which of the following is suitable for the synthesis of bromobenzene from benzene diazonium salts?
explanation:
The conversion of benzene diazonium chloride to bromobenzene using **Cu₂Br₂/HBr** is the **Sandmeyer reaction**:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{N}_2^+\text{Cl}^- \xrightarrow{\text{Cu}_2\text{Br}_2/\text{HBr}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{Br} + \text{N}_2$$

In the Sandmeyer reaction:
- **Cu₂Br₂** provides Br⁻ which replaces the diazonium group
- **HBr** maintains the acidic medium
- N₂ is liberated as a byproduct

Note: Simply using HBr alone does not convert the diazonium salt to bromobenzene — a copper catalyst (Cu₂Br₂) is essential.
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 4

text: Which of the following statements about aniline is FALSE?
options: Aniline is a stronger base than ammonia
Aniline is less basic than methylamine
pKb of aniline is more than that of ammonia
Aniline reacts with bromine water to form a white precipitate
explanation:
**Statement A is FALSE:** Aniline is actually a **weaker base** than ammonia, not stronger.

**Reason:** In aniline, the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is **delocalized into the benzene ring** through resonance (+M effect). This reduces the availability of the lone pair for protonation.

In contrast, in **ammonia (NH₃)**, the lone pair is fully available for donation → ammonia is a stronger base.

Basicity order: $$\text{Methylamine} > \text{NH}_3 > \text{Aniline}$$

**Statements B, C, D are correct:**
- Aniline is less basic than methylamine ✓
- pKb (aniline) > pKb (ammonia) → aniline is weaker base ✓
- Aniline + Br₂(aq) → 2,4,6-tribromoaniline (white precipitate) ✓
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 5

text:
Which of the following statements about sulphonamides is true?

(i) Tertiary amines react with acid chlorides.
(ii) N-ethylbenzenesulphonamide is soluble in alkali.
(iii) N,N-diethylbenzenesulphonamide is soluble in alkali.
(iv) Tertiary amines react with Hinsberg's reagent.
explanation:
**Statement (ii) is correct:** N-ethylbenzenesulphonamide is **soluble in alkali**.

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{SO}_2\text{NHC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{SO}_2\text{N}^-\text{(C}_2\text{H}_5)\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The N–H hydrogen in N-monosubstituted sulphonamides is **strongly acidic** (due to the electron-withdrawing sulfonyl group) → dissolves in alkali.

**Other statements are incorrect:**
- **(i) False:** Tertiary amines do NOT react with acid chlorides (no N–H bond)
- **(iii) False:** N,N-diethyl sulphonamide has NO N–H bond → NOT soluble in alkali
- **(iv) False:** Tertiary amines do not react with Hinsberg's reagent (benzene sulfonyl chloride) — they only react if there's an N–H bond
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 6

text: In the preparation of a primary amine from an alkyl halide, with simultaneous addition of one –CH₂– group to the carbon chain, the reagent used as a source of nitrogen is:
explanation:
**KCN** is the correct reagent.

The reaction proceeds via:
1. **SN2 reaction** of alkyl halide with KCN → **alkyl cyanide (nitrile)**:
$$\text{R-X} + \text{KCN} \rightarrow \text{R-CN} + \text{KX}$$
(This **adds one carbon** to the chain — the CN group adds one C)

2. Reduction of nitrile → **primary amine**:
$$\text{R-CN} + 2\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Na/C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH or LiAlH}_4} \text{R-CH}_2\text{NH}_2$$

This method simultaneously:
- Introduces nitrogen (as CN)
- Adds one carbon (the C of CN) to the chain

Hence, **KCN** is the correct answer.
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 7

text: Acetic acid on heating with NH₃ forms A. When A reacts with LiAlH₄ followed by hydrolysis gives B. When B is heated with chloroform in KOH medium gives C. What are B and C respectively?
explanation:
**Step 1:** CH₃COOH + NH₃ → CH₃COONH₄ → (–H₂O) → **A = CH₃CONH₂ (acetamide)**

**Step 2:** CH₃CONH₂ + LiAlH₄ → (reduction of C=O and C–N) → **B = CH₃CH₂NH₂ (ethylamine)**

Note: LiAlH₄ reduces amides to amines — the carbonyl carbon is reduced to –CH₂–, giving one extra carbon in the amine chain relative to the nitrogen.

**Step 3:** CH₃CH₂NH₂ + CHCl₃ + KOH → **Carbylamine reaction** (primary amine only):
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2 + \text{CHCl}_3 + 3\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{NC} + 3\text{KCl} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
**C = CH₃CH₂NC (ethyl isocyanide)**

**B = CH₃CH₂NH₂; C = CH₃CH₂NC**
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 8

text: If the general formula of oxime and semicarbazone is C=N–Z, what is Z in oxime (I) and semicarbazone (II)?
explanation:
The general formula **C=N–Z** describes these carbonyl derivatives:

**Oxime (I):** Formed from aldehyde/ketone + hydroxylamine (NH₂OH):
$$\text{R-CHO} + \text{NH}_2\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{R-CH=N-OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
In C=N–Z: **Z = –OH** ✓

**Semicarbazone (II):** Formed from aldehyde/ketone + semicarbazide (NH₂–NH–CO–NH₂):
$$\text{R-CHO} + \text{H}_2\text{N-NH-CO-NH}_2 \rightarrow \text{R-CH=N-NH-CO-NH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
In C=N–Z: **Z = –NHCONH₂ (–NHCONH₂)** ✓

Therefore: **I (Oxime): Z = OH; II (Semicarbazone): Z = NHCONH₂**
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 9

text:
For which of the following compounds is Kjeldahl's method NOT used for estimation of nitrogen?

I. Aniline   II. Azobenzene   III. Nitrobenzene   IV. Pyridine
explanation:
**Kjeldahl's method** cannot be used when:
- Nitrogen is present as a **N=N (azo) group**
- Nitrogen is present in a **–NO₂ or –NO group** (N with double bond to O)
- Nitrogen is part of a **heterocyclic ring** (like pyridine)

**Analysis:**
- **I. Aniline (C₆H₅NH₂):** N has no double bond, not in ring → **CAN be estimated by Kjeldahl's** ✗
- **II. Azobenzene (C₆H₅–N=N–C₆H₅):** Has N=N group → **CANNOT** ✓
- **III. Nitrobenzene (C₆H₅NO₂):** N has double bond to O → **CANNOT** ✓
- **IV. Pyridine:** N in heterocyclic ring → **CANNOT** ✓

Kjeldahl's method is **not used for II, III, and IV**.
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 10

text: An organic compound A (C₆H₇N) on reaction with NaNO₂/HCl at 273–278 K followed by warming with water gives B. B reacts with conc. HNO₃ to give C. What is C?
explanation:
**A (C₆H₇N)** = Aniline (C₆H₅NH₂)

**Step 1:** Aniline + NaNO₂/HCl at 273–278 K → **Benzene diazonium chloride** (diazotisation)

**Step 2:** Benzene diazonium chloride + H₂O (warming) → **B = Phenol (C₆H₅OH)**
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{N}_2^+\text{Cl}^- \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O}, \Delta} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{N}_2 + \text{HCl}$$

**Step 3:** Phenol + conc. HNO₃ → **C = 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (picric acid)**
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{2,4,6-trinitrophenol} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The –OH group strongly activates ortho and para positions → trinitration occurs at all three activated positions.
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 11

text: The reaction $$\text{ArN}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + \text{Cu} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{ArCl} + \text{N}_2 + \text{CuCl}$$ is known as:
explanation:
The reaction of a diazonium salt with **copper metal (not cuprous chloride)** in the presence of HCl is called the **Gattermann reaction**:

$$\text{ArN}_2^+\text{Cl}^- + \text{Cu} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{ArCl} + \text{N}_2 + \text{CuCl}$$

**Distinguishing from Sandmeyer reaction:**
- **Sandmeyer:** Uses **CuX salts (Cu₂Cl₂ or Cu₂Br₂)** as reagent
- **Gattermann:** Uses **copper metal** (not salt) with HCl or HBr

Both reactions involve the replacement of the –N₂⁺ group by a halide, but they differ in the copper species used.
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 12

text:
What are A, B and C in the following reactions?

$$\text{Phthalic acid} \xrightarrow{+\text{NH}_3, \Delta} A \xrightarrow{\text{High temperature}} B \xrightarrow{\text{}} C$$
options: A = ammonium phthalate; B = phthalamide; C = phthalimide
A = ammonium phthalate; B = phthalamide; C = 1,2-dicyanobenzene
A = ammonium phthalate; B = phthalamide; C = phthalimide
explanation:
**Step 1:** Phthalic acid + NH₃:
$$\text{o-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{(COOH)}_2 + 2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{o-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{(COONH}_4)_2$$
**A = Ammonium phthalate** (di-ammonium salt)

**Step 2:** Heating ammonium phthalate → dehydration:
$$\text{o-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{(COONH}_4)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{o-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{(CONH}_2)_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
**B = Phthalamide** (the diamide)

**Step 3:** Further heating at high temperature → cyclisation with loss of NH₃:
$$\text{o-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{(CONH}_2)_2 \xrightarrow{\text{high T}} \text{Phthalimide} + \text{NH}_3$$
**C = Phthalimide** (cyclic imide)

**A = Ammonium phthalate; B = Phthalamide; C = Phthalimide**
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 13

text:
What are A and B in the following reaction sequence?

$$\text{Propionitrile} + A \rightarrow B \xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{O}^+} \text{propiophenone}$$
explanation:
Propionitrile (CH₃CH₂C≡N) reacts with a Grignard reagent to form a metalated imine, which upon acid hydrolysis gives a ketone.

For the final product to be **propiophenone (C₆H₅COCH₂CH₃)**:
- The phenyl group must come from the Grignard reagent
- **A = C₆H₅MgBr (phenyl magnesium bromide)**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{C≡N} + \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{MgBr} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{C(=NMgBr)C}_6\text{H}_5}_{B}$$

$$B \xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{O}^+} \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CO-C}_6\text{H}_5 \ (\text{propiophenone}) + \text{NH}_3$$

**A = C₆H₅MgBr; B = CH₃CH₂C(=NMgBr)C₆H₅**
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 14

text:
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text{KCN}} X \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2/\text{catalyst}} Y \xrightarrow{\text{CHCl}_3/\text{alc. KOH}} Z$$

What is Z in the above sequence of reactions?
explanation:
**Step 1:** C₂H₅Cl + KCN → **X = C₂H₅CN (propionitrile)** + KCl
(SN2, CN⁻ attacks as nucleophile via carbon → cyanide product)

**Step 2:** C₂H₅CN + H₂/catalyst → **Y = CH₃CH₂CH₂NH₂ (n-propylamine)**
(Reduction of nitrile to primary amine — adds one CH₂ to the chain)

**Step 3:** CH₃CH₂CH₂NH₂ + CHCl₃ + KOH → **Carbylamine reaction** (primary amine + CHCl₃/KOH → isocyanide):
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{NH}_2 + \text{CHCl}_3 + 3\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{NC} + 3\text{KCl} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Z = CH₃CH₂CH₂NC (n-propyl isocyanide)**
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 15

text:
What are A, B and C in the following reaction?

$$\text{Arene diazonium fluoroborate} \xrightarrow{\text{NaNO}_2/\text{Cu}, \Delta} A + B + C$$
options: A = ArN=NAr; B = N₂; C = NaBF₄
A = ArNO₂; B = N₂; C = NaBF₄
A = ArNO; B = N₂; C = NaBF₄
A = ArF; B = NaN₃; C = BF₃
explanation:
When **arene diazonium fluoroborate (ArN₂⁺BF₄⁻)** is heated with NaNO₂ in the presence of Cu catalyst:

$$\text{ArN}_2^+\text{BF}_4^- + \text{NaNO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu}, \Delta} \text{ArNO}_2 + \text{N}_2 + \text{NaBF}_4$$

Products:
- **A = ArNO₂** (nitrobenzene — the diazonium group is replaced by –NO₂)
- **B = N₂** (nitrogen gas is liberated)
- **C = NaBF₄** (sodium tetrafluoroborate)

This reaction is useful for introducing a –NO₂ group onto an aromatic ring in a specific position that may be difficult to achieve by direct nitration.
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 16

text: The amine which does NOT react with chloroform and ethanolic potassium hydroxide is:
explanation:
The reaction of an amine with CHCl₃/KOH (ethanolic) to form an **isocyanide** is called the **carbylamine reaction**.

**Prerequisite:** The amine must be a **PRIMARY amine** (R–NH₂) — it must have at least two N–H bonds.

- **Option (a): (CH₃)₂CHNHCH₃** — This is a **secondary amine** (has only one N–H bond) → **does NOT react** ✓
- **Option (b): C₆H₅NH₂** — Primary amine → reacts
- **Option (c): C₆H₅CH₂NH₂** — Primary amine → reacts
- **Option (d): CH₃NH₂** — Primary amine → reacts

Secondary and tertiary amines do NOT undergo the carbylamine reaction because they lack the required two N–H bonds.
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 17

text:
What are X and Y in the following reactions?

$$\text{R-CONH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{(1) LiAlH}_4 \text{(2) H}_2\text{O}} \text{RCH}_2\text{NH}_2 \xleftarrow{\text{H}_2/\text{Ni}} Y$$

$$X \xrightarrow{\text{(1) LiAlH}_4 \text{(2) H}_2\text{O}} \text{RCH}_2\text{NH}_2$$
explanation:
The amine RCH₂NH₂ can be formed by two routes:

**Route 1:** RCONH₂ (amide) + LiAlH₄/H₂O → RCH₂NH₂
- So **X = RCONH₂** (amide) ✓

**Route 2 (reverse arrow):** Y + H₂/Ni → RCH₂NH₂
- Catalytic hydrogenation (H₂/Ni) of a **nitrile** gives a primary amine:
$$\text{RCN} + 2\text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2/\text{Ni}} \text{RCH}_2\text{NH}_2$$
- So **Y = RCN** (nitrile) ✓

Both pathways confirm: **X = RCONH₂** and **Y = RCN**

LiAlH₄ reduces both amides and nitriles to primary amines — nitriles give the same product as catalytic hydrogenation.
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 18

text: The order of basic strength of methylamine-substituted amines and NH₃ in aqueous solution is:
explanation:
The basicity of aliphatic amines in **aqueous solution** depends on three competing factors:

1. **+I (inductive) effect** of alkyl groups → increases electron density on N → favours tertiary amine
2. **Solvation effect** → conjugate acid (ammonium ion) stabilised by H-bonding with water → favours primary amine (more N–H bonds to solvate)
3. **Steric effect** → bulky alkyl groups hinder proton approach → disfavours tertiary amine

Net result in aqueous solution:
$$\underbrace{(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{NH}}_{\text{secondary}} > \underbrace{(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{N}}_{\text{tertiary}} > \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{NH}_2}_{\text{primary}} > \underbrace{\text{NH}_3}_{\text{base}}$$

Dimethylamine is the strongest base because it has the best balance of +I effect (2 methyl groups) and solvation (still has 1 N–H for H-bonding).
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 19

text:
What are X, Y and Z in the following reaction sequence?

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2 \xrightarrow{(\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O}/\text{Pyridine}} X \xrightarrow{\text{HNO}_3/\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} Y \xrightarrow{\text{OH}^-} Z$$
options: X = acetanilide; Y = p-nitroacetanilide; Z = p-nitroaniline
X = acetanilide; Y = o-nitroacetanilide; Z = o-nitroaniline
X = acetanilide; Y = o- and p-nitroacetanilide; Z = o- and p-nitroaniline
explanation:
**Step 1:** Aniline + (CH₃CO)₂O/pyridine → **X = Acetanilide (C₆H₅NHCOCH₃)**
(Protection of amino group by acetylation)

**Step 2:** Acetanilide + HNO₃/H₂SO₄ → **Y = p-Nitroacetanilide**
(The –NHCOCH₃ group is less activating than –NH₂ but still ortho/para director; para product predominates due to steric effects)

**Step 3:** p-Nitroacetanilide + OH⁻ (hydrolysis) → **Z = p-Nitroaniline**
$$\text{p-NO}_2\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{NHCOCH}_3 + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{p-NO}_2\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{NH}_2 + \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^-$$

This sequence (protection → nitration → deprotection) is used to obtain **p-nitroaniline** selectively.
name: Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen Item 20
